lens.included_activities: Independent LMM manufacturers of construction, surface-mining, and logging machinery, attachments, wear parts, portable processing equipment, and repeat engineered assemblies sold to external dealers, contractors, mines, rental fleets, or OEMs.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive plants, dealerships and rental fleets without manufacturing, repair-only firms, financing vehicles, shells, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat equipment, attachment, wear-part, replacement, and OEM-program sales, supported by configuration, engineering, warranty, parts, field service, and dealer or fleet relationships.

a.bridge: BLS machinery-manufacturing evidence, not six-digit 333120, shows 63,880 welders and 9,140 engine and other machine assemblers in the displayed industry material, while the three-digit OEWS reports welders at 6.10% of employment. I map configuration, quote and drawing review, CAD assistance, parts identification, scheduling, procurement, warranty triage, technical support, and quality analysis as exposed; welding, machining, assembly, painting, inspection, testing, maintenance, and material handling are less substitutable. The broader proxy may overstate exposure in fabrication-heavy firms.
a.rationale: Complex product and service information creates a moderate AI opportunity, but skilled physical manufacturing remains central.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted task inventories from 12-15 LMM makers split across equipment, attachments, wear parts, and portable processing systems, including current CAD/CAM and ERP use, would materially revise the estimate.
caveats: Product scale and business model vary within the code.

rho.rationale: Configuration, quoting, engineering search, technical documents, parts identification, forecasting, scheduling, and warranty support can be implemented within five years. Safety validation, low-volume variants, legacy systems, field conditions, dealer data, product liability, and physical prototyping constrain realization.
rho.change_evidence: Measured pilots across quote-to-order, engineering change, parts support, warranty triage, production planning, and quality documentation at representative manufacturers would move the range.

e.rationale: Many independent LMM attachment, wear-part, portable-equipment, and niche machinery makers have recurring external customers. Captive OEM plants, dealership-heavy companies, one-project fabricators, extreme OEM concentration, weak aftermarket economics, and non-manufacturing classifications reduce eligibility.
e.change_evidence: Classify the injected 163 candidates using business databases, websites, dealer lists, ownership records, customer mix, repeat revenue, and EBITDA interviews.
caveats: Injected firm count is margin-bridged and estimated.
Classification does not establish independence or recurring external economics.

s5.bridge: Federal Reserve owner-age chartbooks cover small businesses broadly rather than 333120 transfers. I use owner age as succession pressure and discount for internal succession, closures, minority investments, strategic subsidiaries, cyclic earnings, and non-transferable founder or OEM relationships. Consolidation by OEMs and larger attachment suppliers may raise strategic sales.
s5.rationale: A moderate LMM population, succession, and strategic adjacency support transfers, but heavy equipment cycles, product liability, dealer relationships, and customer concentration impede them.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, CapIQ, Grata, Axial, and sector-adviser data on identified 333120 control sales and closures since 2018 would create a denominator-based estimate.
caveats: No public code-specific five-year transfer rate.

q.rationale: Qualification, safety, uptime, installed-base compatibility, dealer relationships, and proprietary designs support retention. Large OEM cost-downs, rental-fleet purchasing, bids, dealer discounts, and cyclical price competition pass part of savings through.
q.change_evidence: OEM and dealer agreements, bid histories, fleet contracts, price-cost bridges, warranty economics, aftermarket margins, and customer interviews would directly test benefit sharing.
caveats: No public contract dataset quantifies pass-through.
Aftermarket wear parts and proprietary attachments likely retain more than build-to-print assemblies.

d5.bridge: Census construction-spending data measure downstream activity rather than machinery quantity, and Federal Reserve production data measure broader 33312. December 2025 construction spending was almost flat year over year at the monthly total while 2025 annual total spending declined 1.4%, with public categories such as highway and water showing growth. I translate this mixed current backdrop into modest five-year real machinery growth from replacement, infrastructure, mining, and specialized projects. The bridge may miss export and logging demand and can overstate machinery purchases if fleet utilization rises instead.
d5.rationale: Replacement cycles and infrastructure, mining, aggregate, and industrial projects support demand, but construction cyclicality, interest rates, rental-fleet utilization, and recent broad spending softness limit the central case.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year unit forecasts by equipment class, rental-fleet capex, dealer inventories, machine age, construction spending by end use, mining capex, and export orders would materially revise the range.
caveats: Construction spending is a downstream proxy, not equipment demand.
Large earthmoving machines and niche attachments follow different cycles.

o.rationale: Retained demand requires an accountable manufacturer to engineer, fabricate, assemble, test, warrant, and support physical machines and wear parts. Autonomy and software change features and utilization but do not eliminate machinery; customers can insource attachments, extend replacement cycles, or buy imports.
o.change_evidence: Contractor, mine, logger, and rental-fleet surveys on utilization, autonomous equipment, repair versus replacement, in-house fabrication, and import sourcing would move the estimate.
caveats: Operator share can shift to large OEMs or foreign suppliers.
This is separate from internal labor automation.

narrative.executive_view: Construction machinery combines durable physical demand, aftermarket relationships, and complex information workflows. AI can improve engineering, quoting, planning, and support, while fabrication and assembly cap labor substitution and end-market cycles shape demand.
narrative.ai_impact: Likely applications include configuration and quoting, drawing and specification review, engineering search, work instructions, parts identification, dealer and warranty support, demand planning, procurement, and quality analysis. Welding, machining, assembly, painting, testing, inspection, maintenance, and field trials remain physical.
narrative.value_capture: Qualification, uptime, safety, installed-base compatibility, proprietary designs, and parts availability support retention. OEM cost-downs, rental-fleet purchasing, dealer discounts, bids, and cyclical competition transmit savings.
narrative.firm_availability: The estimated LMM population is meaningful, especially in attachments, wear parts, and niche systems. Eligibility falls with captive ownership, dealership-heavy models, one-off fabrication, customer concentration, weak aftermarket revenue, and product-liability exposure.
narrative.demand_durability: Construction, infrastructure, quarrying, surface mining, logging, and replacement needs require physical equipment. Demand should remain durable but cyclical, with fleet utilization, interest rates, public spending, commodity capex, and exports driving variance.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Main risks include construction and mining cycles, customer financing, tariffs and steel costs, rental-fleet capex, dealer inventory, warranty reserves, product liability, engineering key persons, and uncertainty in the estimated independent-firm universe.
narrative.principal_driver: Installed-base, parts, dealer, and technical workflows pair recurring physical demand with automatable knowledge work.
narrative.principal_weakness: Heavy physical production and cyclical capital spending limit labor substitution and demand visibility.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: Construction Machinery Manufacturing
sources.supports: Census defines the industry as construction, surface-mining, and logging equipment and lists products including backhoes, bulldozers, rock-drill bits, graders, off-highway trucks, and portable crushing equipment.

sources.title: Machinery Manufacturing: NAICS 333
sources.supports: BLS states complex assembly is inherent in machinery production and lists 63,880 welders and 9,140 engine and other machine assemblers in the displayed broader-industry data.

sources.title: Machinery Manufacturing - May 2023 OEWS
sources.supports: BLS reports welders at 6.10% of broader machinery employment and provides machine-assembler data, supporting the production-heavy task proxy.

sources.title: Construction Spending, December 2025
sources.supports: Census reports 2025 total construction spending 1.4% below 2024 in the displayed year-to-date comparison, with divergent categories such as public highway and water, used as a downstream demand proxy.

sources.title: 2024 Chartbook on Firms by Age of Primary Owner
sources.supports: Federal Reserve chartbook provides small-business owner-age evidence used only as a succession proxy.

sources.title: Industrial Production: Construction Machinery
sources.supports: Federal Reserve publishes a direct 33312 construction-machinery production series, supporting the empirical demand route and indicating monthly 2025 index values around the high 90s in the displayed table.
